
---
title: "Publications and Media"
---

```{=html}
<style>
.title {
  font-size: 0px;
}
</style>
```

## Publications & Media


### Peer-Reviewed Articles

<div class="pub-card">
  <span class="pub-badge badge-peer">Peer-Reviewed</span>
  <div class="pub-title"><a href="https://online.ucpress.edu/cpcs/article-abstract/59/1/104/215179/Micromobilizing-EmotionHow-Feminist-Anti-War?redirectedFrom=fulltext" target="_blank">Micromobilising Emotion: How Feminist Anti-War Resistance Builds Affective Infrastructure in Exile</a></div>
  <div class="pub-authors"><strong>Nugumanova, K.</strong></div>
  <div class="pub-venue">Communist and Post-Communist Studies (2026) &nbsp;•&nbsp; <span style="font-family: var(--font-family-monospace); font-size: 0.75rem; font-weight: 600; color: #1b4332; background: #eaf5eb; padding: 0.15rem 0.4rem; border-radius: 4px;">Scopus</span> &nbsp;<span style="font-family: var(--font-family-monospace); font-size: 0.75rem; font-weight: 600; color: #1a365d; background: #edf2f7; padding: 0.15rem 0.4rem; border-radius: 4px;">Web of Science Core Collection (SSCI)</span></div>
  <details>
    <summary>Abstract</summary>
    <div class="pub-abstract">
      How do grassroots feminist movements convert moral shock into sustained resistance under war, repression, and exile? This article examines the diasporic wing of the Feminist Anti-War Resistance (FAR/FAS), a transnational network of Russian feminists formed in response to Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine in February 2022. Drawing on 56 in-depth interviews with activists in 25 countries, it conceptualizes FAS as an affective infrastructure that joins affect, meaning, and materiality to enable emotional survival and political resistance. Extending Ward's micromobilization model, the article advances the concept of productive mediation—a feminist process that transforms morally charged emotions into politically meaningful action and sustained participation. FAS's repertoire—symbolic performances, embodied signals, grief activism, and care-based practices—creates an emotionally resonant, low-threshold path into and back to collective action in diasporic and authoritarian settings. The analysis shows how alignment of ideology, horizontal and decentralized structure, and ritual lowers entry costs, supports withdrawal and return after burnout, and renders dissent durable. The article contributes to three literatures: affect theory and micromobilization; feminist scholarship on exile and forced migration; and research on transnational feminist infrastructures under authoritarianism. It argues that FAS does not treat emotional labor as ancillary to politics but centers it as a generative force. Rather than merely enduring the emotional fallout of war, FAS channels difficult emotions—grief, guilt, and fear—into collective feminist action, making participation emotionally bearable and sustainable over time.
    </div>
  </details>
</div>

<div class="pub-card">
  <span class="pub-badge badge-peer">Peer-Reviewed</span>
  <div class="pub-title"><a href="https://thefebruaryjournal.org/en/article/85" target="_blank">'Great, Great Sorrow and Eternal Silence': An Experiment in Sociological Dream Interpretation after the 24th of February 2022</a></div>
  <div class="pub-authors"><strong>Nugumanova, K.</strong></div>
  <div class="pub-venue">The February Journal, (01-02), 63-103 (2023)</div>
  <details>
    <summary>Abstract</summary>
    <div class="pub-abstract">
      The article is devoted to reflecting on silence and speaking in the dreams of people in Russia after the 24th of February 2022. Our two-stage analysis of dream narratives and dreamers' comments on them uncovers several key topics related to speaking and silence. Interpreting them with the apparatus of sociology, we conclude that these dreams provide a space for restoring agency that had been lost in real life. Available both in English and Russian.
    </div>
  </details>
</div>


### Working Papers

<div class="pub-card">
  <span class="pub-badge badge-review">Advanced Draft / Under Review</span>
  <div class="pub-title">Moral Responsibility and the Reverse Gender Gap: Political Activism Among Russian Post-2022 Emigrants</div>
  <div class="pub-authors"><strong>Nugumanova, K.</strong>, Kamalov, E., and Sergeeva, I.</div>
  <div class="pub-venue">Advanced Draft</div>
  <details>
    <summary>Abstract</summary>
    <div class="pub-abstract">
      This paper examines gender differences in political activism among Russian anti-war emigrants who left the country following the full-scale invasion of Ukraine in 2022. Drawing on original survey data (N = 1,734) and 68 in-depth interviews, the study analyzes participation across four repertoires of anti-war activism: street protests, digital activism, financial support for independent Russian NGOs and media, and assistance to Ukrainian refugees. Contrary to classical theories predicting lower political participation among migrant women, the findings reveal a reverse gender gap: women demonstrate higher overall engagement, particularly in beneficiary-oriented and digital forms of activism, while no significant gender difference emerges in street protest participation. The paper argues that this pattern is not primarily explained by political socialization or migration-related constraints, but by stronger war-related moral appraisals among women, especially perceived moral responsibility and support for reparative action. The study contributes to research on political participation, gender and migration, and the emotional dynamics of transnational activism under authoritarianism.
    </div>
  </details>
</div>

<div class="pub-card">
  <span class="pub-badge badge-working">Working Paper</span>
  <div class="pub-title">Transnational Repression and Engagement: Evidence from a Panel of Russian Exiles</div>
  <div class="pub-authors">Kamalov, E., <strong>Nugumanova, K.</strong>, and Sergeeva, I.</div>
  <div class="pub-venue">Working Paper (to be presented at APSA (2026), ECPR (2026))</div>
  <details>
    <summary>Abstract</summary>
    <div class="pub-abstract">
      Transnational repression (TNR), authoritarian states' efforts to monitor, intimidate, and punish their citizens abroad, has become an escalating threat to opposition communities in exile. Existing scholarship argues that TNR can be highly effective at demobilizing and atomizing diasporas by raising the perceived costs of political "voice" from abroad. Yet most evidence comes from qualitative studies, limiting our ability to estimate TNR's overall effect on political participation. Moreover, we know little to what extent political withdrawal is driven primarily by direct victimization or also by secondhand exposure, i.e. learning that repression has affected one's social circle. This study addresses these gaps using a unique panel survey of Russian citizens who left Russia after the full-scale invasion of Ukraine in 2022 in opposition to the Putin regime. We document diaspora members' exposure to various forms of TNR, including legal repression, sentences in absentia, and less visible practices such as interrogation at border control, restrictions on consular services, and proxy repression of exiles' relatives. We implement a difference-in-differences design to estimate how direct and indirect exposure to TNR influences political participation and political remittances. We further disaggregate effects by type of transnational repression and examine whether impacts persist over time. By providing the first large-N, longitudinal estimates of TNR's behavioral consequences in a major contemporary exile community, the study clarifies if diaspora remains resilient in the face of repression.
    </div>
  </details>
</div>

<div class="pub-card">
  <span class="pub-badge badge-working">Working Paper</span>
  <div class="pub-title">Destination Preferences in Secondary Migration Among Politically Induced Migrants from Authoritarian States: Evidence from Conjoint Experiment</div>
  <div class="pub-authors">Kamalov, E., <strong>Nugumanova, K.</strong>, and Sergeeva, I.</div>
  <div class="pub-venue">Working Paper</div>
</div>

<div class="pub-card">
  <span class="pub-badge badge-working">Working Paper</span>
  <div class="pub-title">The Gendered Opportunity Structure of Exile: How Host-Country Contexts Shape Migrant Political Participation</div>
  <div class="pub-authors"><strong>Nugumanova, K.</strong></div>
  <div class="pub-venue">Working Paper </div>
</div>

<div class="pub-card">
  <span class="pub-badge badge-working">Working Paper</span>
  <div class="pub-title">Two Percent of Trust: Methodological Innovations for Retaining Fearful Respondents in Panel Surveys</div>
  <div class="pub-authors">Kamalov, E., <strong>Nugumanova, K.</strong>, and Sergeeva, I.</div>
  <div class="pub-venue">Working Paper</div>
</div>

<div class="pub-card">
  <span class="pub-badge badge-working">Working Paper</span>
  <div class="pub-title">New Russian Migration to Latin America: Mobile Middle Classes, Flight from Illiberalism, and the Formation of New Urban Communities</div>
  <div class="pub-authors">Ruseishvili, S., Kamalov, E., <strong>Nugumanova, K.</strong>, and Sergeeva, I.</div>
  <div class="pub-venue">Working Paper</div>
</div>


### Reports & Policy Briefs

<div class="pub-card">
  <span class="pub-badge badge-working">Analytical Report</span>
  <div class="pub-title"><a href="https://outrush.io/report_march_2025_eng" target="_blank">On the Move: Mobility, Integration, and Dynamics of Russian Emigration in 2022-2024</a></div>
  <div class="pub-authors">Kamalov, E., <strong>Nugumanova, K.</strong>, and Sergeeva, I.</div>
  <div class="pub-venue">OutRush (March 2025)</div>
  <details>
    <summary>Description / Summary</summary>
    <div class="pub-abstract">
      This paper investigates the lives of Russians who emigrated after February 2022, based on longitudinal data from the OutRush project. The primary focus is on emigrants' adaptation processes, their political activity, and their connections with Russia. Findings indicate that emigrants—predominantly highly skilled specialists in IT, culture, and science—contribute to the economic and cultural life of their host countries, while simultaneously experiencing complex and ambivalent feelings toward Russia.
    </div>
  </details>
</div>

<div class="pub-card">
  <span class="pub-badge badge-working">Analytical Report</span>
  <div class="pub-title"><a href="https://outrush.io/report_latam" target="_blank">From Russia to Latin America: Integration, Challenges, and Aspirations of Russian Post-2022 Emigrants</a></div>
  <div class="pub-authors">Kamalov, E., <strong>Nugumanova, K.</strong>, and Sergeeva, I.</div>
  <div class="pub-venue">OutRush (July 2025)</div>
  <details>
    <summary>Description / Summary</summary>
    <div class="pub-abstract">
      This OutRush report explores the characteristics, experiences, and motivations of Russian emigrants in Latin America, focusing primarily on those who left after Russia's invasion of Ukraine in February 2022. Based on responses from 938 participants, the report highlights that post-war emigrants primarily moved due to political reasons, safety concerns, and the threat of mobilization. Most emigrants chose Latin American countries due to political freedom, ease of residency, and naturalization opportunities.
    </div>
  </details>
</div>

<div class="pub-card">
  <span class="pub-badge badge-working">Expert Report</span>
  <div class="pub-title"><a href="https://outrush.io/report_artists_2024" target="_blank">Mapping and Gathering Russian Artists and Cultural Workers in Exile</a></div>
  <div class="pub-authors"><strong>Nugumanova, K.</strong></div>
  <div class="pub-venue">Report for the European Commission (May 2025)</div>
  <details>
    <summary>Description / Summary</summary>
    <div class="pub-abstract">
      This report highlights the need for targeted policies and programs that address both the contributions and the vulnerabilities of cultural workers in exile. Appropriate support from host countries can help facilitate their integration, make use of their skills, and encourage cross-cultural exchange. The findings of this study are intended to inform policymakers, cultural institutions, and support organizations in developing effective and practical strategies to support this group of exiled cultural workers.
    </div>
  </details>
</div>

<div class="pub-card">
  <span class="pub-badge badge-working">Analytical Report</span>
  <div class="pub-title"><a href="https://outrush.io/report_january_2024" target="_blank">One and a Half Years Later: Progress and Barriers in the Integration of Russian Emigrants / Полтора года спустя: прогресс и барьеры в интеграции российских эмигрантов</a></div>
  <div class="pub-authors">Kamalov, E., Sergeeva, I., Zavadskaya, M., <strong>Nugumanova, K.</strong>, and Kostenko, V.</div>
  <div class="pub-venue">OutRush — Analytical Report, Wave 3 (January 2024)</div>
  <details>
    <summary>Description / Аннотация</summary>
    <div class="pub-abstract">
      OutRush is a longitudinal study of Russian emigrants who left after February 24, 2022. The project tracks changes in emigrants' lives, the dynamics of their relationships with host societies, and their ties with their country of origin. Three survey waves have been conducted; this report is based on the third wave (May–July 2023). A total of 10,871 respondents from over 100 countries participated in the OutRush surveys. Economic integration trends show that two-thirds of emigrants who previously worked for Russian companies have since left them and joined international or local firms. Before departure, 43% of respondents worked for Russian companies; by September 2022 this figure had dropped to 17%, and by summer 2023 — to 13%.<br><br>
      Проект OutRush — это лонгитюдное исследование российских эмигрантов, уехавших после 24 февраля 2022 года. Цель исследования — отследить изменения в жизни эмигрантов, динамику их отношений с принимающими обществами и страной исхода. Всего в опросах OutRush приняли участие 10 871 респондентов из более чем 100 стран.
    </div>
  </details>
</div>


### Non-Peer-Reviewed Publications

<div class="pub-card">
  <span class="pub-badge badge-peer">Journal Article</span>
  <div class="pub-title"><a href="https://css.ethz.ch/content/dam/ethz/special-interest/gess/cis/center-for-securities-studies/pdfs/Russian_Analytical_Digest_316.pdf" target="_blank">Men's Words, Women's Work: Exploring the Reverse Gender Gap in Post-2022 Russian Emigration</a></div>
  <div class="pub-authors"><strong>Nugumanova, K.</strong></div>
  <div class="pub-venue">Russian Analytical Digest, 10(316), 13-18 (2024). DOI: 10.3929/ethz-b-000692533</div>
  <details>
    <summary>Description / Abstract</summary>
    <div class="pub-abstract">
      Are men truly more engaged in politics or do they just talk a good game? Since February 2022, over a million Russians have fled political repression, and among them, a surge in female activism has emerged, challenging traditional gender roles. Despite having fewer resources than men, Russian women are more politically and civically active abroad. This "reverse gender gap" has been marked in interviews with exiled activists and consistently observed in the OutRush survey. Traditional predictors of political participation do not fully explain this gap, which seems to be driven by war-induced emotions. I demonstrate that efforts and rewards are unfairly distributed between the genders: while women engage in intellectual work, they are, inter alia, disproportionately burdened with emotional labor, as well as the "domestic" and invisible tasks within the movements, whereas men focus primarily on high-visibility roles.
    </div>
  </details>
</div>

<div class="pub-card">
  <span class="pub-badge badge-media">Media Essay</span>
  <div class="pub-title">How do you sleep during the war? / Как тебе спится во время войны?</div>
  <div class="pub-authors"><strong>Nugumanova, K.</strong></div>
  <div class="pub-venue"><a href="https://posle.media/language/en/how-do-you-sleep-during-the-war/" target="_blank">Posle Media</a> (English) / <a href="https://posle.media/kak-tebe-spitsya-vo-vremya-vojny/" target="_blank">После Медиа</a> (Russian), 23 March 2023</div>
</div>

<div class="pub-card">
  <span class="pub-badge badge-media">Media Essay</span>
  <div class="pub-title"><a href="https://republic.ru/posts/107912" target="_blank">'I Had a Dream About Putin': What Wartime Dreams Tell Us About Reality / «Приснился Путин». Что сны времен войны рассказывают нам о реальности</a></div>
  <div class="pub-authors"><strong>Nugumanova, K.</strong></div>
  <div class="pub-venue">Republic, 8 April 2023 (RU, English version available on request)</div>
</div>


### Podcasts & Public Interviews

<div class="pub-card">
  <span class="pub-badge badge-media">Expert Interview</span>
  <div class="pub-title"><a href="https://republicmag.io/posts/116686" target="_blank">'People Who Live in Fear Tend to Have Very Realistic Dreams' / «Люди, которые живут в страхе, видят очень реалистичные сны»</a></div>
  <div class="pub-authors"><strong>Nugumanova, K.</strong></div>
  <div class="pub-venue">Republic (2024)</div>
</div>

<div class="pub-card">
  <span class="pub-badge badge-media">Radio Podcast</span>
  <div class="pub-title"><a href="https://www.sbs.com.au/language/russian/ru/podcast-episode/how-post-war-immigrants-from-russia-live-in-latin-america/d5ns3frol" target="_blank">How Russian Post-War Immigrants Live in Latin America</a></div>
  <div class="pub-authors">Kamalov, E., <strong>Nugumanova, K.</strong>, and Sergeeva, I.</div>
  <div class="pub-venue">SBS Radio (2026)</div>
</div>

<div class="pub-card">
  <span class="pub-badge badge-media">Radio Podcast</span>
  <div class="pub-title"><a href="https://www.sbs.com.au/language/russian/ru/podcast-episode/double-cancel-russian-cultural-workers-in-exile/man4obfzd" target="_blank">Double Cancel: Russian Cultural Workers in Exile</a></div>
  <div class="pub-authors"><strong>Nugumanova, K.</strong></div>
  <div class="pub-venue">SBS Radio (12 Feb 2025)</div>
</div>

<div class="pub-card">
  <span class="pub-badge badge-media">Podcast Episode</span>
  <div class="pub-title"><a href="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_9P0T40goSE" target="_blank">'What Do Russians' Dreams Say About Them?' / «Что о россиянах говорят их сны?»</a></div>
  <div class="pub-authors"><strong>Nugumanova, K.</strong></div>
  <div class="pub-venue">Meduza, "What Happened" Podcast (2 Jan 2024)</div>
</div>

<div class="pub-card">
  <span class="pub-badge badge-media">Media Interview</span>
  <div class="pub-title"><a href="https://syg.ma/@feminist-anti-war-resistance/uiekhavshiie-rossiianie-bolieie-fieministski-nastroieny-sotsiologhi-iz-proiekta-outrush-o-novoi-volnie-rossiiskoi-emighratsii" target="_blank">'Russians Who Left Are More Feminist-Minded': Sociologists from the OutRush Project on the New Wave of Russian Emigration / «Уехавшие россияне более феминистски настроены»</a></div>
  <div class="pub-authors">Kamalov, E., <strong>Nugumanova, K.</strong>, and Sergeeva, I.</div>
  <div class="pub-venue">Sigma (15 Sept 2023)</div>
</div>

<div class="pub-card">
  <span class="pub-badge badge-media">Media Interview</span>
  <div class="pub-title"><a href="https://paperpaper.ru/komu-to-snilos-chto-dyhanie-stalo-pla/" target="_blank">'Someone Dreamed That Breathing Had Become Paid': What Sociologists Learned from 900 Dreams of Russians After February 24 / «Кому-то снилось, что дыхание стало платным»</a></div>
  <div class="pub-authors"><strong>Nugumanova, K.</strong></div>
  <div class="pub-venue">Paper (30 Nov 2022)</div>
</div>

<div class="pub-card">
  <span class="pub-badge badge-media">Alumni Interview</span>
  <div class="pub-title"><a href="https://eusp.org/news/studentka-eu-karolina-nugumanova-rasskazala-ob-obuchenii-na-programme-socialnye-instituty-i-praktiki" target="_blank">European University Student Karolina Nugumanova on Studying in the 'Social Institutions and Practices' Program</a></div>
  <div class="pub-authors"><strong>Nugumanova, K.</strong></div>
  <div class="pub-venue">European University at Saint Petersburg (18 March 2021)</div>
</div>


### Public Speaking & Lectures

<div class="pub-card">
  <span class="pub-badge badge-working">Conference Paper</span>
  <div class="pub-title"><a href="http://www.academicbridges.sbs/?page_id=290&lang=ru" target="_blank">In the Footsteps of Emigrants: In Search of a New World (Academic Bridges)</a></div>
  <div class="pub-authors"><strong>Nugumanova, K.</strong></div>
  <div class="pub-venue"> <em>War doesn't have a woman's face? Political behaviour and civic engagement of Russian post-war migrants: the gender dimension from the OutRush Longitudinal Study</em> (June 2023)</div>
</div>

<div class="pub-card">
  <span class="pub-badge badge-working">Invited Guest Lecture</span>
  <div class="pub-title"><a href="https://eusp.org/projects/gender-likbez" target="_blank">Gender Differences in Political and Civic Activism Among Russian Migrants</a></div>
  <div class="pub-authors"><strong>Nugumanova, K.</strong></div>
  <div class="pub-venue">Public Lecture at the Summer School GENDER-LIKBEZ 4.0, European University at St. Petersburg (April 2023)</div>
</div>

<div class="pub-card">
  <span class="pub-badge badge-working">Invited Lecture</span>
  <div class="pub-title">Public science lecture series on fiction and documentary film</div>
  <div class="pub-authors"><strong>Nugumanova, K.</strong></div>
  <div class="pub-venue">Guest speaker at cultural spaces in St. Petersburg (2022)</div>
</div>

<div class="pub-card">
  <span class="pub-badge badge-media">Political Vlog</span>
  <div class="pub-title"><a href="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=M5xh4NUUk_4" target="_blank">Elections: What Was That, and Will It Happen Again? / Выборы: что это было и будет ли это еще?</a></div>
  <div class="pub-authors"><strong>Nugumanova, K.</strong></div>
  <div class="pub-venue">Politics Anew / Политика Заново, Issue 1 (2021)</div>
</div>


### References & Acknowledgements

<div class="pub-card">
  <span class="pub-badge badge-working">Media Coverage</span>
  <div class="pub-title"><a href="https://www.thedial.world/articles/nenews/issue-25/ukraine-russia-war-dream-recordings" target="_blank">'Anya, Wake Up! They Started a War!'</a></div>
  <div class="pub-venue">Mireille Juchau, The Dial (4 Feb 2025)</div>
</div>

<div class="pub-card">
  <span class="pub-badge badge-working">Report</span>
  <div class="pub-title"><a href="https://outrush.io/memo_january_2024" target="_blank">A Year and a Half in Exile: Progress and Obstacles in the Integration of Russian Migrants</a></div>
  <div class="pub-venue">Sergeeva, I., Kamalov, E. (15 Jan 2024)</div>
</div>

<div class="pub-card">
  <span class="pub-badge badge-working">Preprint</span>
  <div class="pub-title"><a href="https://osf.io/preprints/socarxiv/3dnsv" target="_blank">Voice after Exit? Exploring Civic Activism among Russian Migrant Communities in Eurasia after February 24, 2022</a></div>
  <div class="pub-venue">Zavadskaya, M., Sergeeva, I., & Kamalov, E. SocArXiv (23 Dec 2023). DOI: 10.31235/osf.io/3dnsv</div>
</div>

<div class="pub-card">
  <span class="pub-badge badge-working">Media Coverage</span>
  <div class="pub-title"><a href="https://www.museumofdreams.org/dreaming-under-russian-terror" target="_blank">Dreaming Under Russian Terror</a></div>
  <div class="pub-venue">Raimondo Lanza, Museum of Dreams (27 June 2023)</div>
</div>

<div class="pub-card">
  <span class="pub-badge badge-working">Report</span>
  <div class="pub-title"><a href="https://drive.google.com/file/d/17MawIn4q9ONNBbRBU2gN1NUItj2Zro4N/view" target="_blank">Six Months in Exile: A New Life of Russian Emigrants</a></div>
  <div class="pub-venue">Kamalov, E., Sergeeva, I., Zavadskaya, M., & Kostenko, V. SocArXiv (20 May 2023)</div>
</div>

<div class="pub-card">
  <span class="pub-badge badge-working">Media Coverage</span>
  <div class="pub-title"><a href="https://paperpaper.ru/shulman-snitsya-chashe-chem-kadyrov-kak/" target="_blank">'Shulman Appears in Dreams More Often Than Kadyrov': How Female Sociology Students Study Russians' Dreams After February 24</a></div>
  <div class="pub-venue">Paper (8 April 2022)</div>
</div>
